text: The surgeon uses a laparoscopic approach to perform a mesh patch closure, securing the mesh with trans-fascial sutures, tacks, or a combination of both.
The surgeon performs an incisional hernia repair using primary sutured closure, demonstrating the technique of directly suturing the fascial defect.
The surgeon closes the fascial defect using a non-absorbable polypropylene V-lock suture, visibly securing the tissue edges together.
The surgeon uses a V-lock suture with barbs along its length to maintain tension and securely close the fascial defect.
The surgeon demonstrates the use of a V-lock suture with a loop at the end, facilitating the start of suturing without the need to tie a knot.
The surgeon uses multi-site robotic instruments with wristed articulation to perform precise suturing during the incisional hernia repair.

The surgeon precisely places the needle through the fascia while using the trailing arm of the robotic instrument to maintain tension on the polypropylene V-lock suture line, aiding in the secure closure of the fascial defect.
The surgeon uses adequately sized needles with the suture to take generous bites of the fascia, ensuring a secure repair of the fascial defect.
The surgeon advances the suture to close the fascial defect, with the barbs on the V-lock suture maintaining tension and keeping the defect closed during the process.
The surgeon continues suturing along the fascial defect using a V-lock suture, progressively closing the defect until it is completely sealed.
The surgeon releases carbon dioxide to decrease intra-abdominal pressure, allowing the margins of a larger fascial defect to approximate.
The surgeon identifies the hernia along the lateral edge of the mesh from a previous hernia repair and begins to reincorporate the mesh into the current repair process.
The surgeon uses needle holders with wrist articulation to place sutures at various angles, demonstrating flexibility that is challenging to achieve with straight laparoscopic instruments.
The surgeon tightens the suture line to eliminate any gapping between the suture passes, ensuring a continuous closure of the fascial defect.
The surgeon advances the suture line a centimeter or two beyond the apex of the defect, then reverses direction to make additional passes through the fascia, ensuring secure closure.
The surgeon uses a V-lock suture with barbs, which provide resistance to secure the suture line without the need for a knot at the end.
The surgeon makes a few additional passes of the suture through the fascia in the opposite direction, creating enough friction to maintain tension on the suture line repair.
The surgeon completes the primary closure of an incisional hernia using a multi-site robotic technique. The suture is divided, and the needle is removed from the abdominal cavity.
